detailed_analysis: Both scoped files are small mixin modules that contribute strategy methods to the layer-2 use case AdoptionEngine (architecture §4). extender.py (64 lines) renders the canonical to newly-participating tools; conflict_resolver.py (80 lines) implements N-way conflict resolution by argmax(mtime) with archive-of-losers. Both are well under the 300-line module limit, every function is under the 40-line limit, names follow the two-word/role-plus-domain convention, and type annotations are present on all parameters and returns. No hardcoded secrets, no injection vectors, no bare-except swallowing, no mutable default arguments, no no-op implementations. The Dependency Rule holds: both import only inward (canonical, rendering, state, sync_types, archive, agentic_tool_spec) and never reference a concrete tool name. NFR-15 secret_policy is enforced downstream inside the parse/render IO layer (mcp_server_io, invoked via render_to_agentic_tool and tool_io.parse), so these strategy mixins correctly do not re-implement it. Archive-before-overwrite (I-3 / NFR-01) is honoured: the conflict path archives every loser's bytes before _sync_from_agentic_tool writes, and the extend path archives pre-existing shared-keyed-map slots via _archive_prior_slot_results. Only minor smells found: a magic-string '.json' fallback default and an unqualified canonical.get('name') log value. No CRITICAL or MAJOR findings.

findings.problem: Note: this method lives in adopter.py, not the scoped files, but it is the archive helper invoked by extender._extend_to_new_tools (extender.py:57). Within the scoped extend path the fallback extension '.json' is an inline magic string with no named constant; flagged for completeness as it governs archive filenames on the extend path. Out of strict scope.

verdict_summary: Both adoption strategy mixins are clean, correctly layered, and spec-conformant; only a minor out-of-scope magic-string smell in the archive helper they call.
